title: How to migrate from DurableAgent to WorkflowAgent
value: Migrates durable agents to AI SDK with durable streaming and approvals
tradeoffs: Most agent code carries over unchanged, but differences are concentrated in imports, stream output, and a few renamed options.
WorkflowAgent moves tool approval from a manual hook to a tool property.
WorkflowAgent exposes only `stream()`; `generate()` is removed.
limitations: `@ai-sdk/workflow` requires the `ai` package and `zod` as peer dependencies, plus the `workflow` package for the runtime.
WorkflowAgent writes raw `ModelCallStreamPart` chunks; UI chunks require `createModelCallToUIChunkTransform()`.

`WorkflowAgent.stream()` expects `ModelMessage[]`; `UIMessage[]` must be converted with `convertToModelMessages()`. `result.messages` must be converted back with `convertToUIMessages()` for UI rendering.`maxSteps` is replaced by `stopWhen`; same step-count limit uses `isStepCount(10)`.`,`experimental_output` is renamed to `output`, read from `result.output`.`,`needsApproval` is specific to `WorkflowAgent`; for `generateText`, `streamText`, and `ToolLoopAgent`, use `toolApproval` instead.`,`runtimeContext`, `toolsContext`, and anything returned from `prepareStep` must be serializable.`,`Do not put functions, class instances, symbols, database clients, or SDK clients in context.`,
comparisons: vs DurableAgent: package/class changes from `@workflow/ai/agent` to `@ai-sdk/workflow`; stream output changes from `UIMessageChunk` to `ModelCallStreamPart`; step limit changes from `maxSteps` to `stopWhen`; structured output changes from `experimental_output` to `output`; tool approval moves from a hook in `execute` to `needsApproval`; server-side context splits from `experimental_context` to `runtimeContext` and `toolsContext`; `collectUIMessages`/`result.uiMessages` are removed and replaced with `result.messages` plus conversion; `generate()` is removed in favor of `stream()`; client transport import moves to `@ai-sdk/workflow`; `onStepFinish` becomes `onStepEnd`.
vs generateText/streamText/ToolLoopAgent: `needsApproval` is not used; `toolApproval` is used instead.

title: How Fluid compute pricing compares to EC2, Fargate, and EKS

title: How to use Vercel Container Registry

Companies plan limits: Hobby 10/50/1,000, Pro 1,000/10,000/10,000, Enterprise 5,000/50,000/50,000 for repositories/images/tags. Storage costs $0.10 per GB per month.

vs the built-in runtime in Sandbox: custom images start with your packages and binaries already in place instead of the built-in runtime.

title: Comparing MySQL, PostgreSQL, and MongoDB
value: Compares database trade-offs for choosing the right data store
tradeoffs: MySQL is designed primarily for vertical scaling.
MySQL primarily uses a primary-replica model, and a primary failure can introduce a single point of failure.

PostgreSQL materialized views improve performance for complex, infrequently changing, read-heavy queries, but they must be refreshed to stay current when underlying data changes once persisted results are saved as a table only after the initial query. MongoDB does not conform to ACID properties by default, trading data accuracy guarantees for flexibility/performance tailored to app needs.
limitations: MongoDB supports multi-document transactions only in v4.0+.
MySQL replication can introduce a single point of failure if the primary server fails.
PostgreSQL statement-based replication can cause inconsistencies with non-deterministic functions like NOW() or RANDOM().
comparisons: vs MySQL: PostgreSQL is more flexible when it comes to scaling and can be scaled both vertically and horizontally.
vs MySQL: PostgreSQL supports more advanced indexing techniques, including partial, expression-based, and materialized views that can be indexed directly.
vs MySQL: PostgreSQL offers a range of replication solutions, including synchronous and asynchronous options, plus Warm Standby and Hot Standby high-availability options.

title: Next.js on Vercel vs Netlify
value: Side-by-side fine print for choosing Next.js hosting on Vercel vs Netlify
tradeoffs: On Netlify, Next.js support depends on the OpenNext adapter rather than native framework support.
Netlify middleware runs on Edge Functions, so Node.js middleware there cannot use C++ add-ons or filesystem access.

Netlify buffers request and response payloads at 6 MB, which can affect larger payloads and streams. 20 MB streamed responses are the cap on Netlify, and background functions return an empty 202 immediately, so they can't stream or return results to the triggering visitor.
limitations: Netlify caps synchronous function execution at 60 seconds and the limit is not configurable.
Vercel Functions run up to 300s on Hobby and 800s on Pro and Enterprise; supported Node.js and Python runtimes can opt into 1800s (30 minutes, beta) with Fluid Compute.
Netlify Background Functions run up to 15 minutes but return an empty 202 response immediately, so they can't stream or return results to the visitor who triggered them.
comparisons: vs Netlify: Vercel has native Next.js support maintained by Vercel, while Netlify support comes via the OpenNext Netlify adapter.
vs Netlify: Vercel Functions use Fluid compute with one instance handling many concurrent requests, while Netlify Functions are serverless and each instance handles one request at a time.

vs Netlify: Vercel allows middleware on Vercel Functions including Node.js runtime middleware, while Netlify middleware runs on Netlify Edge Functions (Deno). Build setup is zero-config on both, but Netlify uses the adapter and may need netlify.toml for customizations like pinning the adapter version. Landing pages or routes using `export const runtime = 'edge'` run in the functions region on Netlify rather than at edge locations, with no functionality missing. On Vercel, they run on Vercel Functions as expected for middleware. Vercel supports global cache purges in about 300ms for on-demand or tag-based revalidation, while Netlify purges take a few seconds to propagate. Vercel Functions have up to 4 GB per function, while Netlify defaults to 1024 MB and can be configured up to 4096 MB on credit-based plans. Netlify supports Next.js 13.5 and later via the adapter, while Vercel supports the framework natively and new capabilities land there first.

Fluid compute is used by default

tradeoffs: Caching the response for 1 hour adds staleness versus live data
Using the Vercel cache driver requires a specific storage path

title: What can I do about Vercel Functions timing out?
value: Helps reduce timeouts with longer-running, better-scaling functions
tradeoffs: Optimized concurrency can make multiple invocations share one instance
Background work may continue after the initial response using waitUntil/after
For even longer-running functions, you may need to override default duration settings
limitations: Fluid Compute is currently supported only for Node.js and Python runtimes
Vercel Functions can time out when tasks run too long or upstream services fail to respond
If a function never returns an HTTP response, Vercel waits until the maximum duration then times out
comparisons: vs traditional serverless functions: Fluid Compute offers higher default and maximum duration limits
vs traditional serverless functions: Fluid Compute reduces cold starts and improves resource utilization
vs traditional serverless functions: Fluid Compute supports background processing after the initial response

title: What can I do when I run into build output limits with Next.js on Vercel?
value: Helps large static sites stay under build-output limits by using fallback pages.
tradeoffs: Using fallback pages initially shows visitors a loading state while the page renders in the background.
The blocking option delays the entire response until the page is fully rendered.
The guide says fallback can negatively impact SEO, though it notes modern search engines can crawl JavaScript.
limitations: The guide says the limit it refers to is the maximum number of build outputs.
For large sites that generate more than 1000 pages, it recommends considering fallback pages.
comparisons: vs fallback: true: fallback: 'blocking' delays the entire response until the page is fully rendered.

title: What is a Large Language Model (LLM)?
value: No explicit sales value stated in the guide.
tradeoffs: LLMs are not retrieval systems; they generate answers using context and probability.
Some models are further fine-tuned for domain-specific data, which is presented as an added step rather than a universal default.
Some models are refined using human feedback after training, adding another post-training process.
comparisons: vs traditional retrieval-based systems: LLMs don't retrieve answers, they generate them using context and probability.

title: What is an LLM Tool?
value: Connect an LLM to real-world actions via function calls.
tradeoffs: LLMs can’t fetch real-time data, query databases, or modify state on their own.
limitations: Node.js 18+ and pnpm are required for the example.
An AI Gateway API key is required for the example.
The sample weather bot uses mock data rather than a real weather API.
comparisons: vs a text-generating model: a tool-enabled LLM can interact with the real world via registered functions.
vs running the app without tool use: the full loop lets the model choose tools, generate arguments, and receive results before answering.

title: What is Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
value: LLMs answer from retrieved docs, not guesses
tradeoffs: Direct LLM calls can be inaccurate or hallucinate when docs are outdated
Sending complete documentation can exceed the LLM context window
Processing complete documentation for every question is prohibitively expensive and slow
limitations: Even models with large context windows like 128k tokens struggle with complete documentation sets that can contain millions of tokens
Chunks that are too small lack context
Chunks that are too large dilute search relevance by mixing different topics together
comparisons: vs calling the LLM directly: direct calls may return inaccurate answers or hallucinate from older training data
vs including complete documentation in the prompt: RAG avoids context-window overflow and the high cost/latency of processing everything each time
vs keyword matches: semantic search retrieves conceptually related content, not just keyword matches

title: Streaming in web applications
value: Stream data and AI output progressively to reduce perceived wait time.
tradeoffs: Chunk sizes can be out of your control, so code must handle chunks of any size.
Flow control may require pausing the stream or buffering data until the client is ready.
limitations: Backpressure can lead to memory overflow errors or data loss when the client’s memory reaches capacity.
In local development, chunk sizes won’t be impacted by network conditions because no network transmission is happening.
Web Streams API is supported in most major web browsers and popular runtimes, such as Node.js and Deno, but the guide does not claim universal support.
comparisons: vs traditional methods: wait for the entire response before the client can see content, whereas streaming sends chunks as soon as they’re ready.
vs standard function responses: standard responses send the full payload at once; streaming sends pieces of the payload over time.
vs non-streaming AI replies: you can show each word or generated text progressively instead of waiting for the complete response.

title: What is Vercel's Green Energy Policy?
value: Serverless architecture can reduce energy use and carbon emissions.
tradeoffs: Vercel relies on multiple cloud providers to deliver services to end users.
comparisons: vs traditional servers: serverless runs on-demand, while traditional servers stay powered even when idle.

title: What is WorkflowAgent?
value: Durable, resumable agents with step retries and approvals
tradeoffs: Requires running inside Workflow SDK runtime with 'use workflow' and 'use step'
WorkflowAgent.stream() writes to a writable instead of returning a consumable stream
Tool execute functions without 'use step' lose durability guarantees
limitations: WorkflowAgent does not have a generate() method
WorkflowAgent.stream() expects ModelMessage[]; use convertToModelMessages first
WorkflowChatTransport needs a POST endpoint returning x-workflow-run-id and a GET {api}/{runId}/stream endpoint with startIndex
comparisons: vs ToolLoopAgent: in-memory vs workflow runtime; lost on crash vs survives restarts; manual retries vs automatic per step; toolApproval vs needsApproval; generate() available vs not available; stream() returns a consumable stream vs writes to a writable

vs DurableAgent: WorkflowAgent lives in @ai-sdk/workflow, writes lower-level model stream parts instead of UI chunks, uses shared stop conditions instead of maxSteps, uses output instead of experimental_output, and replaces experimental_context with runtimeContext + toolsContext

title: What is Vercel's policy regarding load testing deployments?
value: Clarifies when load tests are allowed and how to coordinate them safely
tradeoffs: Load testing can create extra invocations and third-party usage, causing unwanted additional usage and billing
Fluid Compute requires monitoring GB-hours in addition to invocations
Fluid Compute pricing differs from standard functions
limitations: Load testing is only permitted on Enterprise plans
Load testing before an Enterprise contract must be discussed with the sales manager

If no prior warning is given, load testing is likely a breach of the fair usage policy and source IPs may be blocked shortly after starting due to abnormal traffic patterns','Source IPs must be < 1000','Enterprise customers can deploy to multiple regions','For Fluid Compute, allow 2-3 minutes for initial scaling to reach optimal performance
comparisons: vs Hobby and Pro plans: serverless function concurrency limit is 30,000 on Hobby and 100,000 on Pro; Enterprise is higher
vs standard functions: Fluid Compute has dynamic resource allocation and enhanced scaling capabilities
vs static landing pages / SSR pages: pages calling Serverless Functions can fail if function capacity is exceeded

title: What should I do if I receive a 503 error on Vercel?
value: Reduce 503 throttling by lowering function load and scaling pressure.
tradeoffs: Increasing concurrency may strain your upstream provider further.
Burst scaling may take several minutes during substantial traffic increases.
Using cache-control headers adds caching behavior, even if only briefly.
limitations: Default concurrent executions limit is 30,000 for Pro and 100,000 for Enterprise.
Vercel scales function instances only up to 30,000 concurrent executions for Pro and 100,000 for Enterprise, with the option for extended concurrency.
After the initial burst, Vercel scales by a maximum of 500 concurrency per minute.
comparisons: vs manual intervention: Vercel automatically scales function instances based on demand.
vs uncached responses: cache-control headers can reduce requests reaching your function and improve response times.
vs dynamic generation: ISR generates static pages on-demand to help manage high-traffic events.

tradeoffs: Community discussions are public, so others can see and respond to your request.

limitations: Vercel does not guarantee a response.

Feedback submitted via the feedback field is not shared publicly.

title: Why are my Build Logs loading infinitely and not showing up?
value: Build logs can be blocked by antivirus streaming inspection.
tradeoffs: Bitdefender Encrypted web scan requires responses to finish before scanning, which conflicts with streamed build logs.
You may need to disable a security feature or add an allowlist exception to view logs.
limitations: Reports received so far were from customers using Bitdefender on Windows.
If your antivirus has no relevant settings and disabling/uninstalling it fixes the issue, you must contact the antivirus vendor first.
If the issue reproduces without any antivirus software, contact Vercel through vercel.com/help.
comparisons: vs a different computer or device without antivirus installed: use it to confirm whether the antivirus is causing the issue
vs disabling/uninstalling antivirus software: if logs appear afterward, the antivirus is the cause

title: Why are my Vercel builds queued?
value: Explains why builds queue and what to buy or upgrade for more build concurrency.
tradeoffs: More concurrent build slots cost extra for Pro teams.
On a single Git branch, newer pushes can cancel queued builds to favor the latest commit.
limitations: Hobby accounts cannot increase concurrent build slots.

Plan limits are Hobby (1), Pro (12), Enterprise (Custom).","Only one commit on a single branch can be built at a time, even with more slots purchased.","If a previous commit is already building on the same branch, later commits queue.","After the first build completes, only the most recent queued commit deploys; other queued builds are cancelled.","Enterprise users can use Urgent On-Demand Concurrency to skip the Git branch queue for specific runs, but project-level On-Demand Concurrency still applies."],

title: Why aren't commits triggering deployments on Vercel?
value: Explains why commits may not trigger deployments and how to fix it.

If you connect the same Git account to a second Vercel account, the existing link on the first account is removed.
limitations: For private repositories connected to a Team project, you must be a member of that Pro team or the Owner of the Hobby team for deployment to trigger successfully.
GitHub requires collaborator access to set up the repository connection; GitLab requires Maintainer; Bitbucket requires Admin.
It is not possible to have more than one Vercel account linked to the same Git account.

Vercel Functions do not have a persistent filesystem you can read from and write to
Environment variables must be defined the same way in Preview and Production environments

title: Why am I no longer receiving email after adding my domain to Vercel?
value: Add MX records so email keeps working after moving nameservers to Vercel.
tradeoffs: You may need to add multiple MX records, depending on your provider.
If your email provider isn’t in the preset list, you must add records manually.
DNS changes can take a few minutes to become globally available.

Google Workspace is used only as an example in the guide; other providers require their own required arguments.
A TXT verification record may need to be placed at the root domain, with an empty `name` field.

title: Why has my account or deployment been paused?
value: Explains why an account or deployment may be paused and what Vercel does next.
tradeoffs: Users may intentionally or inadvertently breach Vercel’s Terms of Service or Fair Use Policy.
Vercel may need to act immediately to pause an account or deployment in certain cases.
If no email outlines next steps is received, Vercel will be unable to discuss the pause further.

title: Why is my deployed project showing a 404 error?
value: Explains why a deployed site can still return 404s.
tradeoffs: Vercel treats every URL as a server-resolved path unless you configure a rewrite for SPAs.
For SPA routing on Vercel, you must add a rewrite in vercel.json.
Misconfigured build settings can lead Vercel to serve files from an empty folder, so expected paths may not exist.
limitations: Vercel can't assume a project is an SPA by default because many frameworks rely on filesystem routing.
If the deployment URL is wrong, has typos, or uses incorrect paths, the page may 404.

If the deployment has been deleted, it will not be available at that URL anymore. You can check deployments in the project dashboard to confirm it exists. It also may require the correct deployment protection access or being logged in to view it.
comparisons: vs filesystem routing: SPAs need a rewrite because Vercel otherwise resolves URLs on the server side.
vs root directory files: for projects categorized as Other frameworks, Vercel prioritizes files in the public folder over those in the root directory.

title: Why is my Vercel Deployment URL being shortened?
value: Explains why autogenerated deployment URLs may be shortened
tradeoffs: Deployment URLs may be trimmed to fit DNS label limits
If team slugs are used, the entire team slug may be trimmed when changes are needed
Project names resembling real domains may be shortened to avoid anti-phishing triggers
limitations: Maximum DNS label length is 63 characters per RFC 1035
Automatically generated Deployment URLs exceeding 63 characters are trimmed from the end
Team slugs can be fully removed if amendments are required
comparisons: vs regular web domains: project names that resemble domains may be shortened to avoid browser anti-phishing protection

title: Why is my Vercel domain not verified?
value: Explains when domain verification may lag due to DNS propagation
tradeoffs: DNS changes can take a while to propagate, so the domain may appear unverified until propagation completes
If a domain is verified but the site is unreachable, local device or network DNS caching may still block access
Manual cache clearing may require restarting the local computer or firewall/router, or using OS-specific commands
limitations: Nameserver changes usually take up to 48 hours to reflect globally
For certain registrars, nameserver propagation can be slower
Cached DNS records are usually cleared automatically after 1-24 hours
comparisons: vs third-party tools like DNS Checker and DNS Map: they can show DNS record state in different regions of the world

title: Why is Vercel CLI asking me to log in?
value: Explains why CLI auth fails and how to renew tokens.
tradeoffs: Tokens expire after 10 days of inactivity.
Keeping a token active requires using the CLI at least once every 10 days.
limitations: Vercel CLI requires login/authentication before accessing user-specific content or admin tasks.
The article states expired tokens can no longer be used and must be replaced.
In CI/CD, you must create a new token from the Vercel Dashboard tokens page.

title: Why is running another CDN on top of Vercel not recommended?
value: Avoid stale-cache errors after production releases
tradeoffs: A 3rd-party CDN may serve stale HTML after a Vercel release
Stale HTML can reference assets that were purged, causing 404s
Vercel cannot control cache behavior of other CDNs
limitations: Vercel’s CDN purges cache globally on each new production deployment
Other CDNs can’t be synchronized by Vercel during releases
Recommended mitigation is a very short TTL or disabling the 3rd-party CDN
comparisons: vs 3rd-party CDN on top of Vercel: Vercel can control its own cache and release flow, but not the other provider’s cache

title: Why must we use the Domain Nameservers method for Wildcard Domains on Vercel?
value: Wildcard domains can be certified automatically via nameservers.
tradeoffs: You must change nameservers for wildcard-domain verification.
Vercel will handle DNS-01 challenges for you automatically, so DNS control shifts to Vercel.
limitations: Wildcard Domains must use the Nameservers method for verification.
DNS-01 challenge is required to generate wildcard certificates.
For certificate renewal, you would otherwise need to update the verification DNS record each time.
comparisons: vs non-wildcard domains: verification uses HTTP-01 instead of Nameservers/DNS-01, and Vercel automatically handles the challenge request.

title: Can I use wildcard domains without switching to Vercel Nameservers?
value: Enable wildcard TLS on non-Vercel DNS setups by delegating _acme-challenge to Vercel.
tradeoffs: Creating the _acme-challenge NS record may prevent other hosting providers from issuing certificates for their service.
This setup uses a workaround instead of switching the apex domain’s nameservers.
limitations: Wildcard domains require Vercel nameservers by default to issue and auto-renew TLS certificates.
This method should not be used for apex wildcard domains (example given: *.example.com).
If you cannot change your apex domain’s nameservers, use NS records on the _acme-challenge subdomain as a secondary option.
comparisons: vs changing apex domain nameservers: NS records on _acme-challenge let you keep your current DNS provider’s nameservers while delegating only certificate validation to Vercel.
vs other hosting providers: delegating _acme-challenge to Vercel may prevent them from issuing certificates for their service.

title: Using Headless WordPress with Next.js and Vercel
value: Headless WordPress on Vercel serves cached pages fast and updates without redeploys
tradeoffs: Decoupling frontend and CMS means managing separate, independently scalable pieces of infrastructure
The frontend can show the last statically generated content from cache while WordPress is down
Content updates are checked only on the revalidation interval during ISR
limitations: WordPress plugins like WPGraphQL are needed to generate a GraphQL API
WPGraphQL’s default GraphQL endpoint is `/graphql`
The example query fetches the first 20 posts
comparisons: vs WordPress REST API: plugins like WPGraphQL can generate a GraphQL API with zero configuration
vs redeploying: ISR lets you update content without needing to redeploy your website
vs WordPress-only architecture: if WordPress has downtime, the Vercel-hosted frontend continues serving traffic without interruption

title: How can I migrate a site to Vercel without downtime?
value: Migrate a domain to Vercel without downtime by preconfiguring DNS and SSL first.
tradeoffs: Continue using your current DNS provider during setup instead of switching nameservers immediately.
You may need to manually copy DNS records or download a zone file to recreate the current configuration.
If you use the dashboard, you must copy TXT records into your DNS provider and click Verify to issue the SSL certificate.
limitations: DNS changes may take a while to propagate.
The TTL is generally 5 minutes for domains, so migration usually takes about 5 minutes to propagate to Vercel.
Nameserver changes can take up to 48 hours to propagate.
comparisons: vs Cloudflare: the guide shows querying the future DNS authority directly, e.g. @example.ns.cloudflare.com instead of Vercel's @ns1.vercel-dns.com.

title: How can I do a "Zero Downtime" DNS migration to Vercel?
value: Helps move DNS hosting with minimal or no downtime.
tradeoffs: You must clone and verify records before switching nameservers.
You may need to copy records manually or use a zone file.
If you bought the domain from Vercel, you must contact Support for the nameserver change.
limitations: Nameserver changes can take up to 48 hours to propagate.
comparisons: vs Cloudflare: example future DNS authority uses @example.ns.cloudflare.com for verification.
vs another DNS provider: you can verify records against the future DNS authority before switching.
